Viscoelasticity of biofilms and their recalcitrance to mechanical and chemical challenges

evidence_entities.text: over 60% of all human infections treated by physicians are due to biofilms
bacteria adhering to a substratum surface and co-adhering with each other embed themselves in a matrix of extracellular polymeric substances (EPS)
Penetration of chlorhexidine into oral biofilms increased with increasing relative importance of the slow and decreasing importance of the fast relaxation element
Gentamicin has been shown to have enhanced killing in P. aeruginosa and Escherichia coli biofilms in vitro and in vivo in rabbits after ultrasound treatment

notes: This review paper focuses on the viscoelastic properties of biofilms and their role in resistance to mechanical and chemical challenges
The paper discusses biofilms as a major cause of human infections and their resistance mechanisms
Various measurement techniques and treatment approaches for biofilm-related infections are described
